My longtime relationship with antiperspirants was like that of a social smoker and cigarettes: I knew they were ultimately harmful and comforted myself with the notion that I would one day just.stop.smoking.

So when I discovered that antiperspirants actually did make me more susceptible to cancer, I was confused. Why would one product make me more susceptible to cancer than another? And so I did what any sensible person would do: I checked out the science.

When I first read about antiperspirants in the late 1980s, I thought the science was pretty clear. Researchers studied more than 250,000 women, and found that the products made women, on average, 6% more likely to develop breast cancer. While there are some caveats, the results were pretty damning.

But as it turns out, there's a lot more to this story than you might have guessed. The best evidence for antiperspirant carcinogens comes from a study that followed 1,619 men and women — all of whom had recently used antiperspirant. In the study, about two-thirds of men and one-third of women developed breast cancer. (The difference is similar to what researchers found in an earlier study that followed more than 5,000 women in France.)

Hate group says the accused Florida school shooter did paramilitary exercises and got a rifle from members.

A gunman who shot dead 17 people at a Florida high school last week had been active in a white supremacist organisation, according to a report by the Southern Poverty Law Centre (SPLC) released on Saturday.

US law enforcement officials have said Nikolas Cruz, 19, was a member of the group the Aryan Nations and had been active with the group for more than two years, although the centre said that he may not have actually been affiliated with the movement.

The group said in an online statement that it “has never received any direct or indirect financial support from the Aryan Nations” but that it knew Cruz from various “circumstances” that included his involvement in paramilitary drills and the group’s Facebook page.

The report said the group had also received money from several major donors and “liked” posts on its Facebook page calling for violence against non-white people.

According to the report, the gunman in the attack at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School was known to members of the group and bought a rifle from one of its members before becoming a member.

Can I Run Python From Notepad?

This is an excerpt from the book “Python on Windows” by David H. Vaughan-Nichols. In the book, he talks about how to run the Python interpreter from notepad.

With Python 2.7 and up, you can run Python from Notepad. This means you can start your python program from the command line (and have it run), without having to install or unpack Python.

For example:

Python

python -m pip install python2

First, you need to install Python. It’s available for free at http://www.python.org/download/

Next, you need to install the Python 2.7 interpreter. For that, you can use the command pip install 2.7 . This will install Python 2.7, as well as the Python interpreter.

If you want to install 2.7, you will need to install the pkg package manager (http://packages.python.org/).

Everyone talks about the technical talent crunch in Boston (and other hubs). I have never heard an actual explanation of why there is a shortage of developers in the tech sector.
I think it's the combination of all the companies that have moved to Boston and the lack of startups that are looking for developers.  There are a lot of companies that have to build from scratch, and they're not in the tech sector.
The big one here is IBM. They have a huge presence in Cambridge, and are hiring like crazy, but they also have a ton of development staff in Boston.
Yeah IBM has been hiring like crazy.  It's also really hard to get into IBM positions.  I've always wanted to work at them, but they seem to be in such a tight market it's tough.  My uncle used to work for them, and he quit when he realized just how hard it was to get into the program.
Oh yeah. It's almost impossible to get into a programming role at IBM. My friend worked on a computer vision project where they were building a facial recognition system, and it looked like they were only hiring 1 or 2 people per year into it.

CRAIG Joubert, the referee at the centre of the controversy over Scotland’s narrow Rugby World Cup quarter final loss to Australia last year, has revealed why he ran off the pitch at.

Scotland captain John Barclay is set to meet England head coach Eddie Jones in Tokyo on Monday to discuss their controversial World Cup quarter final defeat.

Barclay has been under fire for his decision to charge Australia lock Israel Folau with a dangerous tackle in the quarter final – which saw him sent off.

And ahead of a planned meeting with Jones, Barclay has detailed his explanation for his actions.

Speaking to BBC Sport, Barclay said: “I was getting frustrated, I was getting frustrated trying to get the ball back.

“It was a long game and it wasn’t working for us, so I was trying to get the ball back. I was frustrated because I had a couple of tries disallowed and it made it more difficult.

“I was trying to get the ball back because I knew we were running out of time. I did see that he [Folau] had got to my shoulder, but it was as much to try to get the ball back as anything.

Dr. Rahul Gupta is the senior vice president and chief medical and health officer at March of Dimes.
If some state lawmakers have their way, pregnant women who misuse opioids or other drugs.
The Alabama House is preparing to pass a bill that would make it harder for doctors to prescribe opioids to pregnant women.
"I've seen firsthand how pregnant women are using opioids in an unsafe manner," said Dr. Rahul Gupta, March of Dimes' senior vice president and chief medical and health officer. "They need to be able to get access to safe opioid alternatives."
Gupta says women's bodies are especially vulnerable to opioids, which kill more than 100 people a year in the U.S.
"We know that opioids kill the unborn child because if there's a baby being born from this opioid use, all the oxycodone in the mother's body will go through the placenta into the baby's skull and brain and cause death," he said.
The bill seeks to increase penalties for doctors who prescribe opioids to pregnant women and would make it harder for pregnant women to get their prescriptions refilled.
"It is an incredibly dangerous proposition because we're trying to take away their medication without increasing access to other options," Gupta said.

It's easy to dump shortcuts, files and other miscellany on to your desktop for quick access or for convenience's sake, but before long you can be left with a forest of icons. You might even need to delete some of them in order to keep the ones you really use.

With this guide we're going to show you how to clean up the desktop of Windows 7, 8.1 and 10 using the Task Manager and the File Explorer.

The Process

To begin, you need to find out the process that is causing the problem. You will need to log into the task manager (ctrl-alt-del) and click on the Processes tab at the left side of the screen (the one with the four vertical bars).

If you see anything interesting then you should right-click on it and choose Properties. In the dialog that opens you need to scroll down to the bottom and select Uninstall a program.

You may also want to open the Task Manager and go to the Processes tab. Click on the process you want to uninstall and click on Uninstall.

When you have finished the uninstall, you should see your desktop with the Task Manager on it.

Nicola Sturgeon has assured the family of Sheku Bayoh, the Sierra Leonean man who died on a pavement in Kirkcaldy, Fife, minutes after being detained by police in May, that she will.

Her commitment to the case was described as “truly heartening” by the family of Mr Bayoh, who was detained for 48 hours after being arrested in Kirkcaldy for public order offences.

The 27-year-old was walking near his home in Glenrothes when he was stopped by police for allegedly causing a disturbance with an object.

Mr Bayoh, who had been travelling back from a friend’s house at around 6pm, died on the pavement just minutes later.

His family said the circumstances surrounding his death were “deeply concerning” and the family are still unsure what led to the arrest.

They are also concerned about the lack of training given to police officers, including how to handle someone who is suffering from a mental illness.

Police Scotland said the force continues to review the situation and the investigation into his death is ongoing.

Ms Sturgeon said: “I would like to take this opportunity to offer my sincere condolences to the family of the young man from Sierra Leone, who sadly passed away in Kirkcaldy on 17th May.

The pictures were taken by Steve Troughton, who was working as an unpaid intern at the Portsmouth Evening News covering nightlife for the paper's special music issue.

He was given the job when he was offered a place in the photo department at the paper.

Steve said: 'I was working for Portsmouth Evening News in the photo department and the editor asked me to take over from one of my friends, who had left to do a photography course at the University of Portsmouth.

'I was really excited about it, but I didn't realise there were so many clubs in Portsmouth.

'There was a lot of music in Portsmouth at the time and I took some pictures of people I thought would be interesting.

'A lot of people weren't very impressed with the quality of the pictures because some of them were quite bad.

'I remember one of the pictures which I liked was this guy who was standing in an alleyway and the guy on his left is a policeman, and the guy on the right is a group of kids who are playing football.

Today we examine the XFX Double Dissipation R9 280X video card, comparing it to a fiercely priced custom GeForce GTX 770. We have an exclusive first look at a video card that will feature a pair of active cooling modules that are capable of dissipating a combined 2,000W, ensuring that you get the most out of this high-end graphics card.

In our initial test, our CPU was running at 1.5GHz and our GPU at 800MHz overclock. Our memory clocks were also set to 800MHz, as we wanted to keep the memory as stable as possible when overclocking. In addition, we utilized our Precision Xtreme Memory (PXM) software to make sure that the memory clocks were set correctly.

When it came to power consumption, we ran our tests using a single 6-pin power cable. We tested the power consumption of the GPU and the memory using a single 8-pin power connector, as well. The results were fairly similar, with the GPU only consuming around 50W more than a reference GTX 770.

ZANESVILLE - The Zanesville Community Theatre will open its 56th season this weekend with the award-winning drama, "Of Mice and Men" by John Steinbeck. This is the third year for the ZCT, which has been the longest running community theater in Zanesville.

"This show is one of the best ever written, and we are so proud to have it here in Zanesville," said Director Bob Duchovny. "It's the perfect ending to the season, as it is for most shows."

The show runs through Sept. 30 at the Zanesville Community Theatre, located at 557 S. Main St. Tickets are $12 for adults and $10 for seniors and students.

It was poignant sitting opposite the man whose ambition it had been to remake capitalism for an age of austerity.

In the early hours of Saturday, in a room inside the House of Commons, it was Corbyn and his shadow chancellor John McDonnell who were on the receiving end of the most bitter attacks by their own party, which accused them of being a threat to the very existence of modern British capitalism.

Corbyn, in particular, was repeatedly heckled by backbenchers, as McDonnell faced a chorus of “you’re not fit to be leader” from within his own frontbench.

The Labour leader seemed to know from the start that he would have to fight for his political life on Saturday. He had not sought the leadership of Labour with the intention of challenging Theresa May, and he had no intention of running against the incumbent prime minister.

But in a way, it was the nature of the election campaign that meant that Corbyn had to go head-to-head against his own MPs – a position he had never envisaged.

It was a situation that would make him feel more comfortable than if he had been pitted against May, and it is one in which he has never felt comfortable.

Heritage proponents in Southampton are upset that Bruce County intends to sell the 124-year-old former St. Mary’s school property — a key piece of the county’s history and future development plans for the north end of Southampton.

Suffolk County has received a conditional offer from Heritage Property Trust to buy the 120-acre property at the corner of South Main Street and Highway 17, according to county planning documents. The offer is for $12.5 million, not including taxes.

The county is seeking a $20 million dollar purchase price, plus $2.5 million in planning fees.

The county hopes to use the money to help fund its new development plan for the area, called the “Southampton Town Centre.” The county has said it could spend up to $1.6 billion on the project over the next decade.

“We had a vision for the new town centre, which includes the old St. Mary’s. We now need to find an end user for that site,” said county planning manager Michael Williams.

Cloudflare presents a primer on "anti-patterns" that have transformed IoT devices into ghastly security nightmares.

Cloudflare has released a primer on "anti-patterns" that have transformed IoT devices into ghastly security nightmares, and what companies should do to avoid them or overcome them.

The company's report, titled "Is Your IoT Device Safe from Hackers?," was published on Thursday and highlights the problems associated with common IoT device vulnerabilities, such as the use of unencrypted passwords or default-passwords.

"While IoT devices are often designed to be used by just a few people in a controlled environment, when you are dealing with millions of devices in a wide range of industrial settings, it is easy to forget the responsibility of IoT device owners and developers," Cloudflare noted in its report. "This has led to a spike in the number of IoT attacks and vulnerabilities."

Despite the fact that there are more than 1.4 billion connected devices today, "the IoT landscape is still evolving" and it will continue to get worse without organizations taking steps to prevent attacks, according to Cloudflare.

"According to Cloudflare, although there are over 1.4 billion connected devices today, the IoT landscape is still developing and will continue to deteriorate unless organizations implement measures to thwart attacks."

Digital consumers are being bombarded with one kind of messaging or another. This week, Facebook announced that it would be adding a new feature to its platform that will allow users to send direct messages using their location-based services, like WhatsApp, Signal, and Venmo, and its phone-based services, like Facebook Messenger and Facebook Checkout.

These three companies have been in the midst of rolling out new features for years. But, given how quickly the world has changed in the past few months, it’s become important to provide a more cohesive set of apps for both iPhone and Android users.

Last year, Facebook quietly began testing the “Explore” feature in Messenger for iPhone, which allowed users to view photos and videos from friends, family, and groups that were published on Facebook. That was followed by Android users getting access to the “Explore” feature last October, which allowed them to see posts from people they shared a mutual friend with — but only if they were both playing Fortnite.

Now, with the announcement that Facebook will be adding these new features to Messenger, Twitter, and Venmo, it's clear that the company is determined to make sure that Twitter and Venmo can handle the influx of social media activity that comes with the holiday shopping season.

The UK’s financial watchdog has ordered Standard Chartered to pay £300m in fines for failing to spot suspicious transactions, the Financial Times reports.

HSBC has been fined £250m by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) for breaking EU rules on dealing with criminal suspects, the FT reports.

Following Hurricane Sandy, members of the New York startup community have extended offers of help fellow entrepreneurs and other residents of the tri-state area caught without power and Internet by the storm.

“It’s the best kind of startup community: helping each other out in a time of need,” said Daniel Langer, founder of Manhattan-based mobile insurance company AutoProtect.

AutoProtect has been delivering auto insurance coverage to customers since 2011. Its site helps customers choose an auto insurance policy that meets their needs, then sends them a discount code for the service. Consumers don’t pay for the service until they receive payment from their insurance provider, which is often a few days after the policy is issued.

AutoProtect has so far been able to deliver about $10 million worth of discounts through its site, Langer said. Customers save money because they never have to pay to use the site; they just call or visit the company’s offices to request the discount.

As of Monday, AutoProtect had provided discounts to almost 70,000 people, with more than 10,000 of those customers coming from outside New York state, Langer said.

I am a chemist with a specialty in flavor science. My work involves testing for various kinds of flavorants and developing new flavors for our customers to use in their products. My job is not only to taste the products we make, but to also test them for safety and purity.

In my line of work, I get to see a wide range of products, from food to personal care products. I can also be exposed to a lot of different substances. Today’s food production requires a lot of chemicals, a lot of food products, as well as a lot of different products that are being consumed.

As a result, I am always exposed to a lot of different flavors. I can also taste them.

I am a member of the Flavor Institute, a worldwide group of leading flavor experts who have worked together on research and development projects related to flavor. I am also a member of the International Flavor Association (IFA).

The flavor community has an important role in food safety, especially in food products that are often exposed to people or animals.

Tottenham Hotspur striker Son Heung-min was overwhelmingly chosen as the athlete of the year among Korean sportspeople.

The 28-year-old, who scored four goals in the Champions League last season, won the award at the Sports Journalists Association of Korea’s annual awards ceremony on Sunday (local time).

He was also named the Korean Football Association’s player of the year for the second successive season.

Son beat BTS member Suga and basketball player James Wiseman to the prize.

Earlier this month, Son announced he would be leaving Tottenham to join Italian club Atalanta as he wants to play in Europe.

He said that he “felt a lot of pressure” after completing a £19m move to Spurs from Red Bull Salzburg in January 2017.

He had a successful spell with the Austrian side before moving to north London.

In the 2017/18 Premier League season, Son scored 16 goals in 37 appearances for Tottenham, helping them to finish third in the table, their highest finish since they finished second in the 1998/99 season.

In the pantheon of Santa Barbara artists, perhaps no star shines brighter than Ray Strong (1905-2006).

He was an artist who could paint, sculpt, knit and even play piano.

Ray, as he came to be known, was a very large man, 6-foot-4 and at least 300 pounds.

He was also a highly intelligent man who loved to create, especially when it came to painting.

As for his musical prowess, though he could play piano, it was the violin that really made him sing.

He received the first prize in the California Violin Competition. He taught himself to play by listening to recordings of classical music.

He played classical music, modern music and popular music.

He enjoyed playing with his wife, Doreen, the violinist, and also with her brother, the famous pianist, Joe Zorich.

Ray was born in Los Angeles, the son of a Jewish family who lived in a small apartment on the sixth floor of the Hotel Federal.

His father, Julius, owned a jewelry store, and his mother, Edna, was a seamstress.

The Facebook data privacy and Russian election interference scandals show that artificial intelligence is still not up to many critical jobs in the technology sector.

The lack of diversity was a major issue when Twitter acquired the AI startup DeepMind last year, and even now the company remains predominantly white and male.

The problem extends beyond Twitter, where two of the three CEO candidates for Facebook's AI division are white males. Facebook has more than 75% white male employees, according to a recent report by the research firm DiversityInc.

And it's not just at Facebook that companies are failing to hire enough people from underrepresented groups.

"A key reason for the lack of diversity in artificial intelligence is that it's a relatively new field, and its practitioners have been overwhelmingly white men," said Andrew Ng, a professor of computer science at the University of Toronto who is an advisor to the AI industry on diversity issues. "But there is no question that the field has a long way to go."

Facebook declined to comment for this article, but noted in a statement that the company is committed to improving diversity within its ranks.

Ng also pointed out that social media companies are still trying to figure out how to best use automation to make AI more efficient.

The 3-year-old boy squirmed in his mother’s arms, an IV drip unspooling from his wrist, his skin flush with a pale pink rash. The boy had been in this position before.

In the two years since he arrived at the clinic where he was being treated for malnutrition and dehydration, he had spent most of his time lying on a hospital bed, hooked to monitors and intravenous drips as his body struggled to hold on to food.

The child had a chronic disease called hyperthyroidism, which causes an extra thyroid gland to develop from the base of the neck, making it too large. It can cause bone fractures and other health problems.

As his mother waited, the boy was given potassium chloride to help regulate his blood pressure.

“I was scared for him … but I was also very proud of the work I was doing,” said Dr. Laura A. Garcia, who treats children with hyperthyroidism at Columbia University Irving Medical Center.

Dr. Garcia, director of the Center for Endocrinology and Metabolism at Columbia, is one of the world’s leading experts on the condition.

The last few years have proved challenging for offshore drilling major Transocean, which saw its day rates and contracting activity post significant declines. However, the company has managed to retain its leadership position in the offshore drilling industry through the acquisition of rig operator Ensco Plc, and has a significant backlog of work.

Despite such challenges, Transocean’s recent financial results have been impressive. In fact, the company reported another set of results that exceeded expectations.

Transocean’s revenue for the first quarter ended March 31, 2017 was $3.4 billion compared with $3.4 billion in the year-ago period.

Adjusted EBITDA for the first quarter of 2017 was $1.6 billion, up from $1.3 billion in the year-ago period. This included a $400-million gain from the sale of four rigs to an undisclosed U.S. buyer, as well as a $100-million gain from the sale of the S.P.M.C.A. to an undisclosed foreign buyer.

(Source: Transocean)

The company’s capital spending also went up in the first quarter of 2017.

A study led by researcher Lisa Kellogg of William & Mary's Virginia Institute of Marine Science shows that a restored oyster reef can remove up to 10 times more nitrogen from Chesapeake. The research was published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

The results provide an important baseline for environmental restoration efforts in the Chesapeake Bay.

"This is the first time we've looked at the restoration potential of oyster reefs," said Kellogg. "If this is successful, it will be a game-changer."

In one study, a large oyster reef off the coast of New Jersey was found to have removed up to 2.5 million pounds (1.4 million kilograms) of nitrogen per year from the Chesapeake Bay.

That amount was equivalent to the annual production of an oyster farm in the state.

In the other study, researchers found that the Oysterland Program in Virginia has removed more than 12 million pounds (six million kilograms) of nitrogen from Chesapeake.

"This is quite a bit of nitrogen for a single site," said Kellogg. "It also shows that oyster reefs can be used as a tool to remove nitrogen from the bay, even when the sites are new and under-utilized."

Some 190 Nigerian schoolgirls remain missing after being abducted last week, their head teacher has told the BBC - far more than the official figure.

The girls were kidnapped from their boarding school in the northeastern state of Borno on Wednesday by Islamic extremists.

They were taken to an unknown location where they were beaten and kept in a makeshift prison, according to a local government official.

"We are still waiting for them," head teacher Shehu Abubakar said.

"We have been informed that they are not in Boko Haram custody yet."

Mr Abubakar said he was asked for the girls' names by a member of the Federal Government delegation who visited the area.

There had been reports that some of the girls may have been used as sex slaves, but Mr Abubakar said it was impossible to determine if that was true as the girls were still in captivity.

Boko Haram militants have killed thousands of people in an insurgency aimed at creating a caliphate in northeast Nigeria.

Mr Abubakar said five of his students escaped the prison and one student was killed, while another girl died in a stampede during the abduction.

Rabiah Hutchinson is used to being talked about. She’s the woman in charge of all the work at the University of Kansas Health System’s new center for sexual assault victims and survivors.

Hutchinson, who is also the associate director for women’s health, has been in charge of sexual assault response and prevention at KU since 2016.

She is in charge of the university’s Sexual Assault Response and Prevention Center, which opened its doors in June after more than two years of planning, design and construction.

But she’s also a member of the KU Board of Regents, which oversees the university. That puts her in the same room as board members who sometimes ask her to say something they don’t agree with, she said.

The university doesn’t have enough money in its budget to adequately respond to sexual assault cases, Hutchinson said. And that means the university can’t provide counseling services or other resources to victims, making it difficult for them to access justice, she said.

Former President Bill Clinton speaks during a campaign stop for his wife Democratic presidential candidate Hilary Clinton, Jan. 30, 2016, in Fayetteville, Arkansas. (Photo: Mark Humphrey, AP)

Hilary Clinton is campaigning in Michigan on Monday to kick off her 2016 presidential bid, but she will start her day first with a trip to the site of a mass shooting this weekend.

Clinton said Sunday that she plans to meet at noon Monday with survivors and their families of Saturday's shooting at U of M-Dearborn that left two men dead and 15 wounded. The shooting was the third of its kind at a university in the past three years.

"It is so important to get these conversations going, and it is also important to listen, to get their thoughts and feelings," Clinton said Sunday while campaigning in Arizona. "It's not just about them. We need to make sure that this does not happen again."

The shooting at a house party late Saturday night near Dearborn, Michigan, occurred after a male student opened fire with an AK-47 as a large crowd gathered to watch a football game.

Clinton stated on Sunday that she intends to meet at noon on Monday with the survivors and families of the victims from Saturday's shooting at U of M - Dearborn, which resulted in two fatalities and 15 injuries. The shooting was the third of its kind at a university in the past three years . " It is so important to get these conversations going , and it is also important to listen , to get their thoughts and feelings , " Clinton said Sunday while campaigning in Arizona . " It 's not just about them . We need to make sure that this does not happen again . " The shooting at a house party late Saturday night near Dearborn , Michigan , occurred after a male student opened fire with an AK-47 as a large crowd gathered to watch a football game .

A number of Warriors players actively recruited DeMarcus Cousins to the Bay Area, but the newly-signed Warrior can definitely state which one of them presented the worst pitch.

According to the Los Angeles Times, Warriors coach Steve Kerr said Cousins’ pitching made it seem like he was trying to sell a house.

“I was like, ‘Are you serious?’” Kerr said, via the L.A. Times’ Mark Medina. “‘This is an NBA team. You have to get this guy to know who you are.’ They had a pitch in front of him and he looked at his teammates, and he said, ‘Look, I’m not selling anything. This is about me.’ The pitch was, ‘You need a center. There is no other way around it.’”

It’s hard to imagine a worse pitch than that. It’s also hard to imagine a worse situation than Cousins playing for the Warriors.

Kanye West does it again. The Westworld season 2 trailer premiered at the MTV VMAs and was just so amazing.

The second installment of the HBO sci-fi drama is set to premiere on April 29th, and this trailer shows off the new cast members in a very unique way. After the teaser for the first season, it seemed like we’d be getting a very dark and gritty return to the original show’s roots and setting, but this trailer seems to indicate that all is not as it seems.

We start off with a man whose face is covered with a mask. His name is William, he’s played by Evan Rachel Wood (Westworld) and he is the head of security for the park.

He looks much more human than the other hosts you see in the trailer and he’s trying to get to a phone to call 911, which is when the police arrive.

Westworld Season 2 is directed by Jonathan Nolan who helmed the first one. He also wrote the screenplay with Lisa Joy and Jonathan Nolan.

Although the laboriously negotiated and long-delayed Middle East "road map" received a diplomatic boost by the recent intervention of George W. Bush in the Mideast conflict, it's far from clear whether this initiative will lead to anything more than a delay of the inevitable.

The "road map," which was approved by the Palestinians and Jordanians on Sunday, contains only a few details for resolving the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. But the idea that the road map will be the basis for any progress is almost certainly unrealistic.

Bush's intervention comes after several rounds of negotiations between the Israelis and the Palestinians have failed -- including one last spring when the two sides were still far apart.

In its press release announcing the road map, the United States said that it "reaffirms the need for direct negotiations between the parties and calls for an immediate cessation of hostilities" in the area.

The road map says that the parties "will meet with their respective negotiators and continue to negotiate in good faith and in good faith with all other stakeholders." But this is hardly a formula for success.

To be sure, the road map does include a number of important things that could improve the chances for a lasting agreement.

Minnesota State High School League has approved concussion insurance for student athletes starting this fall.

The Minnesota State High School League Board of Directors approved the new insurance policy at its meeting on Tuesday.

“This is a great opportunity to make sure our student athletes and coaches are covered for these serious injuries,” said State Football Coaches Association executive director Brent Hundley. “We encourage all Minnesota high school football teams to take advantage of this new opportunity.”

The League hopes to have all student athletes covered by the policy by the start of the season.

“It’s very important that we have an insurance policy in place so that we can help protect our student athletes from serious injury and accidental head trauma,” said Commissioner Tom Blomstrom.

The policy requires student athletes participating in high school football, basketball and wrestling to be covered under the policy.

The insurance will cover concussions, traumatic brain injuries, traumatic brain injures, neurocognitive disorders and other significant head injuries.

Coaches who do not wish to participate in the insurance program must purchase their own insurance policy.

Thomas L. Friedman became the paper’s foreign affairs Op-Ed columnist in 1995. He joined the paper in 1981, after which he served as the Beirut bureau chief in 1982, Jerusalem bureau chief. He also spent four years as a correspondent for The New York Times in Beijing and Washington. His first book, The World According to Bill Clinton (HarperCollins, 2000), was a New York Times best seller.

In his new role, Friedman will be writing op-eds that reflect on the news of the day, as well as offer his thoughts on major events.

The opinion column will appear in the Sunday edition of the magazine and online at thehill.com.

“I wanted to write about what I know best,” said Friedman, who has been traveling the world on business recently. “I think I can bring the newspaper’s readership a fresh perspective on current events.”

Friedman also had in mind to publish a new op-ed every Sunday, though that date has not been decided upon.

“It is a lot of work, but I am excited,” he said. “It gives me an opportunity to get a lot of writing done in a short period of time.”

The op-eds will also be published as a book.

When it comes to safety in the workplace, organizational culture can be quite literally a matter of life and death. While some might argue that this is an overreaction, a new study by the University of Southern California suggests that the mere presence of an organizational culture can influence employees’ attitudes towards safety.

The study, published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, looked at the impact of various organizational cultures on the perception of safety in the workplace — a topic that has been studied before.

“Organizational climate can have a major effect on people’s perceptions of safety,” said study author Jason M. McManus, associate professor of psychology at USC.

McManus and his co-author, Sarah A. Wilson, assistant professor of corporate communication at the USC Dornsife College of Letters, Arts, and Sciences, analyzed data from the Survey of Workplace Safety Culture (SWCC), which was conducted by the National Safety Council (NSC) between 2005 and 2013.

The researchers found that a worker’s perceptions of safety in the workplace were strongly influenced by his or her organization’s culture.

DJ Premier will be the first artist to release a single on the newly-revived imprint, in a nod to its early-1990s roots. The album’s title track will be released on Friday, April 5, featuring vocals by DJ Premier himself.

Hailing from Brooklyn, NY, DJ Premier is best known for his work with the Beastie Boys (Sabotage, Mike D and Ad-Rock) and as the producer of the 2000 hit “In Da Club.” His debut album, The Essential DJ Premier, was released in 1996 and garnered critical acclaim. His track “Bounce Back” was featured in the 2002 film, The Matrix Reloaded.

DJ Premier is joined on the project by producer/vocalist Mofro, who has worked with artists including Missy Elliott, Erykah Badu, TLC, and Mary J. Blige.

The album, which is produced by Mofro, features 13 tracks that span the globe from Ghana to Miami, New York to Los Angeles.

Fans can pre-order The Essential DJ Premier from all major digital retailers and on iTunes.

New Jersey lawmakers approved a plan to reinstate the individual mandate to try to stabilize the health insurance market.

The House passed the bill on a 245-176 vote Wednesday, sending it to the Senate, where Republicans have vowed to kill it.

Democrats praised the bill's passage but said it doesn't go far enough.

"While this is a good first step, we are disappointed that the Republican plan falls short of what is needed to stabilize our markets and protect consumers," New Jersey Democratic Sen. Cory Booker said in a statement. "But I am encouraged by the progress made to strengthen our laws to protect our seniors and lower-income families."

New Jersey's insurance commissioner is urging the state to maintain its Medicaid expansion under the Affordable Care Act, saying the state would lose $140 million if it did so.

"The only way to ensure that our hardworking taxpayers continue to receive the valuable health care services they need is to retain the Medicaid expansion," Commissioner Robert Mujica said in a news release.

Health insurers face a tough time finding enough healthy people to cover their costs.

Luis Suarez was distraught following Liverpool’s draw with Crystal Palace on Monday, but the Uruguayan can take a measure of solace in the fact that he made history.

The 29-year-old became the most-capped player for his country, surpassing Diego Forlan's record of 109 caps achieved between 2004 and 2013.

Suarez came agonisingly close to scoring the first goal against the Eagles but instead saw his effort saved by Vicente Guaita, before the striker’s penalty was saved again.

Take a look around each team in the North Jersey Interscholastic Conference. The team's standings after 12 games are listed here.

Sophomore Ryan Bowers of West Windsor-Plainsboro South leads his team in scoring with 21 goals and 20 assists to go along with 30 penalty minutes. Bowers, a left winger who is 6-foot-1 and 195 pounds, is tied for second in the NJIC with 11 game-winning goals.

Bowers' brother, junior midfielder and W-P South defender Patrick Bowers, has 14 goals and 13 assists.

"We don't have many players that have scored that many goals," said Ryan Bowers. "We have a lot of forwards, but we also have a lot of midfielders and defensemen, so we have a lot of guys who can score."

The Blue Devils, who are 9-6 in league play, will host Bergen County rival St. Joseph (Mont.) on Saturday at 2 p.m.

West Windsor-Plainsboro South (9-6 in league play) has won three straight games after a tough start to the season that saw them win only two of their first eight games.

"A lot of people doubted us early," said senior midfielder and forward Matt Molloy. "

ANC employee Errol Velile Present who was fired by the ruling party on Wednesday for his alleged involvement in a cash-in-transit heist in Soweto, Johannesburg. Picture: Supplied
The ANC has fired a number of employees allegedly involved in a cash-in-transit heist in Soweto. Picture: Supplied
Johannesburg - The ANC has fired an employee allegedly involved in a cash-in-transit heist that led to the death of a man last month. The party said it had also suspended four others after they allegedly assisted in the heist at a petrol station in Soweto.
The ANC said that employee Errol Velile Present, who was fired for allegedly being involved in the cash-in-transit heist, had been suspended on Wednesday.
"The ANC has suspended four employees for their involvement in the robbery of a supermarket in Soweto," the party said in a statement.
"They include a security guard - who assisted the robbers - and two police officers."
Velile was one of the employees linked to the heist.
He was terminated on Wednesday, but the party did not say why.

Authorities in Bexar County, Texas, on Thursday responded to reports of three individuals with fatal gunshot wounds inside a gated community.

The incident occurred at about 8:46 p.m. at the gated community of Arleta Oaks, located in the 4900 block of River Oaks Drive, according to police spokesman Chris Villanueva.

Bexar County sheriff's deputies and officers from the Bexar County Sheriff's Office were on the scene within two minutes, Villanueva said.

Deputies arrested one man, who is in his 20s, and found two other individuals, who are in their 30s and 40s, with gunshot wounds inside a home.

They were transported to University Hospital in San Antonio, where they died, Villanueva said.

The BCSO's Homicide Unit is investigating the incident. Anyone with information related to the case may call the BCSO's non-emergency line at 210-335-8811.

College Basketball Power 10: Who Runs The Pac-12?

The Pac-12 is the conference to beat in college basketball this season.

With the Big East's demise and the ACC, Big Ten and SEC's seemingly imploding teams, the Pac-12 seems more likely to be the best in the country than ever before.

And it probably should be.

The Pac-12 has consistently produced the nation's top four teams for multiple seasons now, with the top three usually finishing in the top four of the AP Top 25.

Last year, the Pac-12 was so dominant, its only loss came against Oklahoma.

Yes, the rest of the country would love to see the Pac-12's dominance continue, but with the way it operates, it's hard to see how the conference could lose any more than one of its top teams.

I've assembled a list of the top 10 teams in the Pac-12 heading into the 2013-14 season, with the No. 1 team determined by a combination of points scored per game (PPP), assist average per game (A/A) and total points scored per game (TP).
